summary: Yegge argues that code base size is the worst enemy of software development, a minority view he developed after creating a 500,000-line Java game that became unmanageable for a single developer. He contends that Java's static type system and lack of compression facilities (macros, lambdas, declarative structures) forces inevitable bloat, while industry 'solutions' like IDEs and design patterns actually make code bases larger rather than smaller. After years of evaluation, he concludes that switching to a more expressive JVM language like JavaScript (Rhino/ES4), Ruby, or Python could compress a well-engineered Java codebase by 50-75%. He predicts that while Java programmers remain in denial about the problem, alternative JVM languages will eventually become compelling replacements.
key_points: Code base size/bloat is the worst thing that can happen to a project, team, or company - a minority view that only comes from painful personal experience with massive codebases
Java's static type system lacks compression facilities, making bloat inevitable - it's like Tetris where pieces can never fill gaps, only pile up endlessly
Design patterns and refactoring, while organizing code better, actually make codebases larger rather than smaller
IDEs create a circular problem: they encourage static languages that require IDEs, while dynamic features that could compress code defeat IDE capabilities
The solution is switching to more expressive JVM languages (JavaScript/Rhino, JRuby, Jython) that could compress a 500k-line Java codebase to 125k-150k lines
money_quotes: I believe that code weight wrecks projects and companies, that it forces rewrites after a certain size, and that smart teams will do everything in their power to keep their code base from becoming a mountain.
Java is like a variant of the game of Tetris in which none of the pieces can fill gaps created by the other pieces, so all you can do is pile them up endlessly.
Design Patterns was a mid-1990s book that provided twenty-three fancy new boxes for organizing your closet, plus an extensibility mechanism for defining new types of boxes.
If you begin with the assumption that you need to shrink your code base, you will eventually be forced to conclude that you cannot continue to use Java.
An 'X programmer', for any value of X, is a weak player. You have to cross-train to be a decent athlete these days.
Refactoring is like cleaning your closet without being allowed to throw anything away.
People who don't habitually spit will expectorate up to thirty feet, like zoo camels, in order to bespittle you if you even suggest the possibility of using a Lisp or Scheme dialect at your company.
tone: opinionated, conversational, self-deprecating humor mixed with genuine frustration
key_insight: The worst thing that can happen to a codebase is size, and Java's fundamental character makes bloat inevitable - the only solution is switching to more expressive languages that provide real compression facilities.
